content.rendered:
On a wet summer night at Red Rocks in 1981, Bruce playfully covered the Frankie Ford novelty song “Sea Cruise,” complete with bell and horn sound effects.

This was close to a one-off cover–the only other times Bruce performed “Sea Cruise” were at the Naval Academy in Annapolis and in a 1977 guest appearance at the Stone Pony.

Originally written and recorded by Huey Smith, it became a hit when Frankie Ford lifted the instrumental track, recorded his own vocals, and released it in 1959. It went gold and became his biggest hit. Here he is lip-syncing to it on The Dick Clark show that year.

Sea Cruise
First performed: May 28, 1976 (Annapolis, MD)
Last performed: August 16, 1981 (Morrison, CO)

© April 8, 2018

excerpt.rendered: A rainy evening prompts Bruce to cover Frankie Ford’s classic novelty song.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Frankie Fell in Love
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Kicking off Valentine's Week with Bruce's ode to new love and old friends: "Frankie Fell in Love."

jetpack-related-posts.title: Bring It on Home to Me
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One time only: Bruce joins The Wallflowers in a gorgeous cover of Sam Cooke's classic "Bring It on Home to Me."

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Frankie
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Ah, the elusive, romantic epic "Frankie." She's the one that got away multiple times. It took years, but Bruce and the E Street Band finally did right by her in the studio and on stage. Watch and listen inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: State Trooper
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: "State Trooper" is unrivaled as Bruce Springsteen's most harrowing and terrifying song, but it can't match the song that inspired it. Backstory and rare performances inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Sea of Heartbreak
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: "Sea of Heartbreak" first became a hit in 1961, when Don Gibson took it all the way to #2 on the Billboard Hot Country Singles chart. https://youtu.be/A4bo4ByFhLM It charted again eleven years later, when Kenny Price took it to #24. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PDplFSIA0Bw ...and again in 1989, when Ronnie McDowell took it…

jetpack-related-posts.title: Tobacco Road
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One time only (that was recorded, anyway): Bruce, Max, and Tim McLoone cover John D. Loudermilk's "Tobacco Road."
